previous_headings: Correlated Responses
title: Simulating Longitudinal Data with `endpoints`
text: Suppose want simulate longitudinal continuous outcome measured fixed set visits, trajectories differ treatment group. illustration, consider marginal mean model \[ Y_{t}=\beta_0+\beta_{\text {time }} t+\beta_{\text {trt }} x_i+\boldsymbol\beta_{\text {time:trt }} t x_i+\varepsilon_{t}, \quad =1, \ldots, n, \quad t \\{0,1, \ldots, T-1\}, \] \(x_i \\{0,1\}\) treatment indicator residual vector \(\varepsilon_i=\left(\varepsilon_{0}, \ldots, \varepsilon_{, T-1}\right)^{\top}\) follows \[ e_i \sim \mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{V}\right), \quad \mathbf{V}_{t t^{\prime}}=\rho^{\left|t-t^{\prime}\right|} \] \(\mathbf{V}\) standard AR(1) correlation structure: measurements closer time strongly correlated. explicitly, can written \[\mathbf{V}=\left(\begin{array}{ccccc}1 & \rho & \rho^2 & \cdots & \rho^{n-1} \\ \rho & 1 & \rho & \cdots & \rho^{n-2} \\ \rho^2 & \rho & 1 & \cdots & \rho^{n-3} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \rho^{n-1} & \rho^{n-2} & \rho^{n-3} & \cdots & 1\end{array}\right)\] example use: \(\mathrm{AR}(1)\) correlation \(\rho=0.5\), \(T=5\) visits (baseline + 4 follow-visits), constant control-group mean time, non-linear treatment effect across visits, residual SD \(\sigma=0.95\). Step 1: Specify within-subject correlation encode AR(1) structure directly using corr_make(): Step 2: Treat visit “correlated endpoint” Although endpoint_details typically describes distinct endpoints, can also use represent distinct visits single longitudinal outcome. entry specifies marginal mean visit control, plus visit-specific treatment effect. Step 3: Simulate wide data makeData() Wide data returned makeData() (one column per visit) stage standard “wide” format dataset: visit appears separate column (Cont_1, …, Cont_5). key observation collection correlated endpoints can interpreted repeated measures reshape data. Step 4: Convert wide \(\rightarrow\) long long data now looks like typical longitudinal dataset: Longitudinal data reshaping long format Step 5: Validate intended effects correlation verify simulated data reflect intended mean structure AR(1) correlation, fit marginal model using geeM::geem(): Inspecting output model, observe simulation proceeded correctly: True vs. estimated longitudinal treatment effects correlation parameters. (Note: \(\sigma\) marginal residual SD \(\varepsilon_{} \sim N(0,\sigma^2\mathbf{V})\)) now longitudinal data set correct data characteristics use simulation. Finally, can visualize small set subject trajectories:
code: N_visits <- 5  rho <- 0.5  corMat_5_visits <- corr_make(   num_endpoints = N_visits,   values = rbind(     c(1,2,rho), c(1,3,rho^2), c(1,4,rho^3),c(1,5,rho^4),     c(2,3,rho), c(2,4,rho^2), c(2,5,rho^3),     c(3,4,rho), c(3,5,rho^2),     c(4,5,rho)   ) ) sigma <- 0.95 # constant residual error  baseline <- list(   endpoint_type = "continuous",   baseline_mean = 0.45,   trt_effect    = 0,     # no baseline difference   sd            = sigma )  v1 <- list(endpoint_type = "continuous", baseline_mean = 0.45, trt_effect = -0.10, sd = sigma) v2 <- list(endpoint_type = "continuous", baseline_mean = 0.45, trt_effect = -0.15, sd = sigma) v3 <- list(endpoint_type = "continuous", baseline_mean = 0.45, trt_effect = -0.25, sd = sigma) v4 <- list(endpoint_type = "continuous", baseline_mean = 0.45, trt_effect = -0.40, sd = sigma) correlated_visits_wide <- makeData(   correlation_matrix    = corMat_5_visits,   SEED                  = 321,   sample_size_per_group = 2000,   # large n to check asymptotic behavior   endpoint_details      = list(baseline, v1, v2, v3, v4) ) knitr::kable(   head(correlated_visits_wide$data),   caption = "Wide data returned by makeData() (one column per visit)" ) library(tidyverse) correlated_visits_long <-    correlated_visits_wide$data %>%   rowid_to_column('ID') %>% # keep track of patient   gather(.,key="visit",value='Measure',-trt,-ID) %>%   # convert wide to long data   mutate(ID = as.factor(ID),          # clean up names          visit  = case_match(visit,                              "Cont_1" ~ 0,                              "Cont_2" ~ 1,                              "Cont_3" ~ 2,                              "Cont_4" ~ 3,                              "Cont_5" ~ 4)) %>%   arrange(ID) knitr::kable(   head(correlated_visits_long),   caption = "Longitudinal data after reshaping to long format" ) ge1 <- geeM::geem(   Measure ~ factor(visit) * trt,   id     = ID,   corstr = "ar1",   data   = correlated_visits_long,   family = gaussian ) est_effects <- c(   round(summary(ge1)$beta[7:10], 3),  # treatment effects at visits 1-4 (vs baseline)   round(summary(ge1)$phi, 2),         # residual SD (see note below)   round(summary(ge1)$alpha, 2)        # AR(1) correlation estimate )  true_effects <- c(-0.10, -0.15, -0.25, -0.40, sigma, rho)  labels_latex <- c(   "$\\beta_{\\mathrm{trt},1}$",   "$\\beta_{\\mathrm{trt},2}$",   "$\\beta_{\\mathrm{trt},3}$",   "$\\beta_{\\mathrm{trt},4}$",   "$\\sigma$",   "$\\rho$" )  out_tbl <- data.frame(   Parameter = labels_latex,   True      = true_effects,   Estimated = est_effects )  knitr::kable(   out_tbl,   escape = FALSE,   digits = 3,   caption = "True vs. estimated longitudinal treatment effects and correlation parameters." ) set.seed(888) IDs = c(sample(1:4000,8))  correlated_visits_long %>%   filter(.,ID %in% IDs) %>%   ggplot(.,aes(x = visit,y=Measure,group = ID,color = as.factor(trt)))+   geom_line() +   geom_point() +   labs(title = "",        x = "Trial Time",        y = "Measure",        color = "Treatment") +   theme_minimal()+   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1")

title: Simulating Longitudinal Data with `endpoints`
text: alternative way generate longitudinal data using endpoints framework simulate subject-specific random effects use latent quantities inside separate longitudinal data-generating process. approach often natural longitudinal outcome intended follow mixed-effects model, directly represents patient-level heterogeneity random intercepts slopes. example, consider following longitudinal model random intercept random slope: \[ \begin{gathered} y_{ijt} = \underbrace{(\beta_0 + \beta_1 j + \alpha_i)}_{\text{intercepts}} + t\,\underbrace{(\beta_2 + \beta_{1:2} j + \gamma_i)}_{\text{slopes}} + \epsilon_{ijt} \\ \epsilon_{ijt} \sim N(0,\sigma^2) \quad \text{} \quad \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i \\ \gamma_i \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N} \!\left( \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \mathbf{\Lambda} \right) \quad \text{} \quad\mathbf{\Lambda} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_\alpha^2 & \rho_{\alpha\gamma}\sigma_\alpha\sigma_\gamma \\ \rho_{\alpha\gamma}\sigma_\alpha\sigma_\gamma & \sigma_\gamma^2 \end{pmatrix}. \end{gathered} \] : - \(j \\{0,1\}\) indicates treatment group ( \(0=\) control, \(1=\) treatment), - \(\) indexes subjects, - \(t\) indexes time, - \(\alpha_i\) subject-specific random intercept, - \(\gamma_i\) subject-specific random slope. formulation, correlation repeated measurements induced shared random effects rather directly specifying within-subject correlation matrix across visits. Two-stage Simulation Strategy basic idea proceed two steps: Use makeData() simulate subject-level latent variables (e.g., random intercepts, random slopes, additional endpoints TTE outcomes). Use subject-level quantities inside second helper function expands data time generates repeated measurements. approach especially useful longitudinal outcome must correlated endpoints (e.g., hospitalization mortality), since makeData() can generate latent subject-level quantities jointly. Example: Random Effects & Two TTE Endpoints example simulate trial : one fatal TTE endpoint: -cause mortality (ACM), one non-fatal TTE endpoint: hospitalization (HP), one latent random intercept, one latent random slope. first define correlation matrix, encodes dependence among four subject-level quantities: Next, specify two random effects continuous endpoints centered 0: define two TTE endpoints: Now use makeData() generate subject-level dataset, subjects latent random effects generated: stage, simulated dataset contains: TTE_1: -cause mortality, TTE_2: hospitalization, Cont_1: random intercept, Cont_2: random slope. subject-level quantities. remaining step expand data time generate repeated longitudinal measurements. Creating Longitudinal Data , use small helper function : expands subject across time grid, evaluates user-supplied linear predictor, adds Gaussian residual error, optionally censors longitudinal outcome fatal event. example , linear predictor includes: fixed intercept 10, baseline treatment effect, control-group slope \(-\mathbf{0 . 5}\), treatment--time interaction +1 , subject-specific random intercept ( Cont_1 ), subject-specific random slope ( Cont_2 * time ). first generate latent longitudinal outcome without censoring, can verify fixed random effects separately TTE censoring mechanism. Validate Data can now fit linear mixed model verify simulated fixed effects, random effects, induced correlation structure behaving intended: estimated treatment--time interaction, residual variation, random-effects covariance close data-generating values. Next, regenerate longitudinal data, time allowing fatal event (ACM:TTE_1) censor future longitudinal measurements: yields dataset repeated continuous outcome jointly related two TTE endpoints shared subject-level latent structure, additionally truncated fatal event.
code: cor_rf <- corr_make(num_endpoints = 4,                     values = rbind(                       c(1,2,.2), # ACM ~ HP                       c(1,3,.15), # ACM ~ random intercept                       c(1,4,.1), # ACM ~ random slope                       c(2,3,.1), # HP ~ random intercept                       c(2,4,.05), # HP ~ random slope                       c(3,4,.3) # random intercept ~ slope                     ) ) random_intercept <- list(   endpoint_type = "continuous",   baseline_mean = 0,   sd            = 0.5 )  random_slope <- list(   endpoint_type = "continuous",   baseline_mean = 0,   sd            = 0.25 ) ACM <- list(   endpoint_type  = "tte",   baseline_rate  = 1/4,           trt_effect     = log(0.80),   censoring_rate = 1/8,   fatal_event    = TRUE      )  HP <- list(   endpoint_type  = "tte",   baseline_rate  = 1/2,           trt_effect     = log(0.80),   censoring_rate = 1/15,   fatal_event    = FALSE      ) rf_data <- makeData(   correlation_matrix = cor_rf,   sample_size_per_group = 1000,   SEED = 888,   endpoint_details = list(ACM,HP,random_intercept,random_slope),   non_fatal_censors_fatal = TRUE,   enrollment_details = list(     administrative_censoring = 4   ) ) longMaker_lite <- function(data,                            time_sequence,                            linear_predictor,                            residual_error,                            censor_longitudinal_data = FALSE) {      expanded_data <- data %>%     rowid_to_column("ID") %>%     tidyr::expand_grid(time = time_sequence) %>%     mutate(linPred = eval(parse(text = linear_predictor)))      expanded_data$response <- expanded_data$linPred +     rnorm(nrow(expanded_data), mean = 0, sd = residual_error)      # If requested, censor longitudinal measurements after the fatal TTE endpoint   # (here using TTE_1 only, for illustration)   if (censor_longitudinal_data) {     expanded_data$response <- ifelse(       expanded_data$time > expanded_data$TTE_1,       NA,       expanded_data$response     )   }      expanded_data } long_rf_data_test <- longMaker_lite(data = rf_data$data,                                     time_sequence = seq(from=0,to = 2, by = 0.25),                                     linear_predictor =                                       "10 + (0*trt) + (-.5*time) + (1*trt*time) + Cont_1 + (Cont_2*time)",                                     residual_error =1,                                     censor_longitudinal_data = FALSE ) lme4::lmer(   response ~ trt * time + (time | ID),   data = long_rf_data_test ) %>%   summary(correlation = FALSE) ## Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod'] ## Formula: response ~ trt * time + (time | ID) ##    Data: long_rf_data_test ##  ## REML criterion at convergence: 54565 ##  ## Scaled residuals:  ##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max  ## -3.6266 -0.6276 -0.0022  0.6318  3.4589  ##  ## Random effects: ##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev. Corr  ##  ID       (Intercept) 0.25831  0.5082         ##           time        0.06888  0.2625   0.27  ##  Residual             1.00564  1.0028         ## Number of obs: 18000, groups:  ID, 2000 ##  ## Fixed effects: ##             Estimate Std. Error t value ## (Intercept)  9.99614    0.02526 395.682 ## trt          0.01046    0.03573   0.293 ## time        -0.49682    0.01836 -27.061 ## trt:time     0.97304    0.02596  37.477 long_rf_data_final <- longMaker_lite(data = rf_data$data,                                      time_sequence = seq(from=0,to = 2, by = 0.25),                                      linear_predictor =                                        "10 + (0*trt) + (-.5*time) + (1*trt*time) + Cont_1 + (Cont_2*time)",                                      residual_error =1,                                      censor_longitudinal_data = TRUE) knitr::kable(head(long_rf_data_final,16))

title: A User Guide for the `endpoints` Package

code: makeData(correlation_matrix = NULL,   sample_size_per_group = 1000,   endpoint_details = list(     ...     )   )

text: generate count outcomes, use zero-inflated negative binomial distribution log-link function. parameterization arguments follows: baseline_mean: Mean count control group. trt_count: Scalar vector treatment-group mean counts. trt_effect = NULL: Optional scalar vector treatment effects log rate-ratio scale. one trt_count trt_effect may supplied. size: Negative binomial dispersion parameter (larger values reduce overdispersion approach Poisson limit). parameterization used, variance distribution equal \(\mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\phi}\). , size controls \(\phi\). p_zero: Zero-inflation probability. example use can seen : Note: size large, negative binomial approaches Poisson distribution. cases, dispersion parameter may difficult estimate precisely single simulated dataset (e.g., via MASS::glm.nb()), usually practical concern simulation.

title: A User Guide for the `endpoints` Package
text: Gaussian copulas provide flexible way generate multivariate outcomes user-specified marginal distributions dependence. Let \(\mathbf{Z} = (Z_1,\dots,Z_J)^\top \sim \mathrm{MVN}(\mathbf{0}, \Sigma_Z)\). Applying probability integral transform componentwise gives \[ X_j = F_j^{-1}\{\Phi(Z_j)\}, \qquad j=1,\dots,J, \] \(\Phi\) standard normal CDF \(F_j^{-1}\) inverse CDF (quantile function) desired marginal distribution endpoint \(j\). construction preserves chosen marginals induces dependence across endpoints Gaussian copula. Pearson correlation generally preserved nonlinear monotone transformations, makeData() optionally uses numerical calibration (target_correlation = TRUE) find latent Gaussian correlation matrix approximately reproduces requested Pearson correlation matrix observed endpoint scale. Important: target_correlation = FALSE, supplied correlation_matrix used directly latent Gaussian correlation matrix (copula parameter), calibrated Pearson correlation matrix observed endpoint scale. recommended option set TRUE. See Cario & Nelson (1997) details.

text: default, patients enrolled day 0 trial. simulate realistic trial, probabilistic enrollment controlled via following arguments: enrollment_distribution = c("none","uniform","exponential","piecewise"): specifies enrollment-time distribution (default "none"). "uniform", enrollment time \(\mathbf{U}[0,\mathcal{}]\), \(\mathcal{}\) time administrative censoring. "exponential", enrollment time based exponential distribution (see enrollment_exponential_rate). "piecewise", enrollment follows piecewise exponential distribution. option requires specifying piecewise_enrollment_cutpoints piecewise_enrollment_rates (see examples ). enrollment_exponential_rate = NULL: rate exponential distribution enrollment_distribution = "exponential". piecewise_enrollment_cutpoints = NULL: time points defining bins piecewise enrollment pattern. piecewise_enrollment_rates = NULL: rates exponential distribution within time bin. user specifies stochastic enrollment administrative censoring, maximum potential time--study \(\mathcal{} - T_E\), \(T_E\) time enrollment. re-run example , Data admin censoring stochastic enrollment now see patient 3 now censored time 19.72 (instead time 24), enrollment time 4.28. code , observed enrollment curve :  function also supports simple enrollment (uniform) curve:  Stochastic enrollment change underlying event-time generation mechanism (.e., event hazard parameters), can materially change observed follow-time observed event proportions administrative censoring present. Piecewise Enrollment trials, may expect varied enrollment throughout study period (e.g. heavy \(\rightarrow\) light \(\rightarrow\) medium \(\rightarrow\) heavy) due seasonal trends logistic constraints. example, yields following pattern:  transparency, sampler proceeds follows: subject, simulates waiting time enrollment, starting time 0. checks interval interval: randomly drawn exponential waiting time (using interval’s rate) falls within interval, subject enrolls time. , process moves next interval (potentially different rate), adding completed interval’s duration subject’s time. repeats enrollment occurs final cutpoint reached. loop finishes (either enrollment event occurring reaching end intervals), ensures subject’s enrollment time exceed final defined cutpoint. Piecewise Enrollment Proportions Assume 24-month trial target enrollment \(N = 1{,}100\) patients. Suppose want enrollment pattern : 10% enrolled months 0–8, 35% enrolled months 8–16, 55% enrolled months 16–24. achieve piecewise exponential enrollment pattern, solve exponential rates interval using basic probability. First, set cutpoints define three enrollment windows piecewise_enrollment_cutpoints = c(0, 8, 16, 24). creates three time bins: \([0,8),[8,16)\), \([16,24]\). Next, solve \(\lambda_1\) \(10 \%\) patients enroll month 8. exponential waiting time first interval, \[ P(T<8)=1-e^{-8 \lambda_1}=0.10 . \] Rearranging, \[\begin{align*} 1 - e^{-8\lambda_1} &= 0.10 \\ e^{-8\lambda_1} &= 0.90 \\ -8\lambda_1 &= \log(0.90) \\ \lambda_1 &= -\frac{\log(0.90)}{8}. \end{align*}\] Second, solve \(\lambda_2\) \(35 \%\) patients enroll second bin (months \(8-16\) ). probability \[ P(8 \leq T<16)=P(T \geq 8) P(T-8<8 \mid T \geq 8)=e^{-8 \lambda_1}\left(1-e^{-8 \lambda_2}\right)=0.35 . \] Using \(e^{-8 \lambda_1}=0.90\), get \[\begin{align*} 0.90\left(1 - e^{-8\lambda_2}\right) &= 0.35 \\ 1 - e^{-8\lambda_2} &= \frac{0.35}{0.90} \\ e^{-8\lambda_2} &= 1 - \frac{0.35}{0.90} \\ \lambda_2 &= -\frac{1}{8}\log\left(1 - \frac{0.35}{0.90}\right). \end{align*}\] third rate, \(\boldsymbol{\lambda}_3\), affect overall proportion enrolled months 16-24 (remaining \(55 \%\) already determined \(\lambda_1\) \(\lambda_2\) ). However, \(\lambda_3\) affect shape enrollment-time distribution within final bin, set value 0.25. Putting together: double check enrollment scheme: Observed enrollment proportion time period

title: Specification format for endpoint_details used by makeData() — endpoint_details
text: Continuous endpoints generated using Gaussian distribution. Fields: endpoint_type Set "continuous" recognized alias "normal". baseline_mean Numeric scalar giving control-group mean. sd Numeric scalar numeric vector. scalar, common SD used across arms. vector, must length equal total number arms. trt_effect Optional. Numeric scalar numeric vector giving treatment-group mean shifts relative control. scalar used 2-arm trials; otherwise length must K - 1, K total number arms. NULL, data control group generated. NULL arm_mode = "full", trt_effect taken 0 active arms.

title: Specification format for endpoint_details used by makeData() — endpoint_details
text: binary outcomes, event probability modeled logit scale. Fields: endpoint_type Set "binary". baseline_prob Numeric scalar (0, 1) giving control-group event probability. trt_prob Optional. Numeric scalar vector giving treatment-group event probabilities directly. trt_effect Optional. Numeric scalar vector giving treatment-group effects log-odds scale. Exactly one trt_prob trt_effect may supplied. neither supplied, control-binary endpoint generated, -effect endpoint multi-arm setting depending arm_mode.

title: Specification format for endpoint_details used by makeData() — endpoint_details
text: Count endpoints generated using negative binomial zero-inflated negative binomial distribution. Required fields: endpoint_type Set "count". baseline_mean Numeric scalar (> 0) giving control-group mean count. size Numeric scalar (> 0) controlling negative-binomial dispersion. Larger values reduce overdispersion. Set large value approximate Poisson distribution. trt_count Optional. Numeric scalar vector giving treatment-group mean counts directly. trt_effect Optional. Numeric scalar vector giving treatment-group effects log rate-ratio scale. p_zero Optional. Numeric scalar \([0,1]\) giving structural-zero probability. omitted, defaults 0. Exactly one trt_count trt_effect may supplied. parameterization used, variance \(\mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\phi}\), size controls \(\phi\). p_zero parameter independent copula.

title: Specification format for endpoint_details used by makeData() — endpoint_details
text: Time--event endpoints generated exponential event-time distributions. Required fields: endpoint_type Set "time--event" recognized alias "tte". baseline_rate Numeric scalar (> 0) giving control-group exponential event-rate parameter \(\lambda\). mean event time therefore \(1/\lambda\). Optional fields: trt_effect Numeric scalar vector giving treatment-group effects log hazard-ratio scale. censoring_rate Numeric scalar (> 0) giving independent exponential censoring-rate parameter. NULL, random censoring applied. fatal_event Logical scalar. TRUE, endpoint treated fatal censors later TTE endpoints according package's fatal/non-fatal rules.

title: Specification format for endpoint_details used by makeData() — endpoint_details
text: active-arm parameters: scalar values used two-arm trials, vectors must generally length K - 1, K total number arms, continuous sd, vectors must length K, since control-group SD also included explicitly. number arms determined supplied endpoint specifications. Optionally, users may use arm_mode makeData, option used care.

text: Administrative censoring stochastic enrollment affect observed follow-. primarily affects time--event endpoints, may also use possible interim analyses. enrollment model supplied (enrollment_distribution = "none"), subjects treated enrolling time 0. enrollment stochastic administrative censoring enabled, subject receives enrollTime, maximum available follow-reduced \(\mathcal{}\) \(\mathcal{} - T_E\), \(\mathcal{}\) administrative censoring time \(T_E\) subject's enrollment time.

title: Specification format for enrollment_details used by makeData() — enrollment_details
text: administrative_censoring Numeric scalar NULL. non-NULL positive, defines administrative end follow-.     TTE outcome occurring limit administratively censored. enrollment_distribution Character string giving enrollment-time distribution. Must one : "none" subjects enroll time 0. "uniform" Enrollment times drawn \(U(0,\mathcal{})\). "exponential" Enrollment times drawn exponential distribution, truncated \(\mathcal{}\). Requires enrollment_exponential_rate. "piecewise" Enrollment times generated piecewise exponential model user-specified intervals. Requires piecewise_enrollment_cutpoints piecewise_enrollment_rates.  enrollment_exponential_rate Numeric scalar (> 0). Used enrollment_distribution = "exponential".     Gives exponential rate governing enrollment times. piecewise_enrollment_cutpoints Numeric vector strictly increasing cutpoints defining intervals piecewise enrollment.     example, c(0, 8, 16, 24) defines three intervals: [0,8), [8,16), [16,24]. piecewise_enrollment_rates Numeric vector positive exponential rates, one interval defined     piecewise_enrollment_cutpoints. length must     length(piecewise_enrollment_cutpoints) - 1.

title: Specification format for enrollment_details used by makeData() — enrollment_details
text: administrative_censoring supplied enrollment_distribution = "none", subjects assumed enter time 0 remain observation administrative end follow-(unless experience event independently censored earlier).

title: Specification format for enrollment_details used by makeData() — enrollment_details
text: uniform enrollment, subjects enter uniformly interval \([0,\mathcal{}]\). induces heterogeneous follow-, later enrollees less potential follow-time administrative censoring.

title: Specification format for enrollment_details used by makeData() — enrollment_details
text: exponential enrollment, subject entry times sampled exponential distribution truncated administrative censoring time. can used represent rapid early enrollment followed tapering accrual pattern.

title: Specification format for enrollment_details used by makeData() — enrollment_details
text: piecewise enrollment, waiting time enrollment simulated interval--interval. Within interval, exponential waiting time drawn using interval's rate. draw exceeds remaining interval width, process moves next interval. allows users encode enrollment patterns slow--fast accrual, fast--slow accrual, complex piecewise shapes. See vignette("user_guide", package = "endpoints") vignette example use option achieve certain enrollment proportions certain enrollment periods.

title: Simulate trial data with mixed endpoint types, optional Gaussian-copula dependence, and enrollment/censoring features — makeData
text: correlation_matrix numeric correlation matrix specifying dependence structure across endpoints. target_correlation = FALSE, interpreted latent Gaussian scale. NULL, makeData() enters single-endpoint mode, exactly one endpoint must supplied endpoint_details. Typically created corr_make. SEED Optional numeric scalar seed used initialize random-number generator via set.seed(). NULL, current RNG state used. sample_size_per_group Integer scalar integer vector giving sample size per arm. scalar, sample size used arms. vector, must length \(K\), \(K\) total number arms, including control. endpoint_details non-empty list endpoint specification lists, one per endpoint. sub-list defines marginal distribution arm-specific parameters one endpoint. See endpoint_details complete schema, supported endpoint types, detailed examples. enrollment_details named list controlling enrollment administrative follow-. Fields include: administrative_censoring Numeric scalar. non-NULL, follow-administratively censored time. enrollment_distribution Character. One "none", "uniform", "exponential", "piecewise". enrollment_exponential_rate Numeric scalar rate exponential enrollment enrollment_distribution = "exponential". piecewise_enrollment_cutpoints Numeric vector cutpoints defining intervals piecewise enrollment. piecewise_enrollment_rates Numeric vector rates, one per interval, piecewise exponential enrollment. See enrollment_details full details recommended parameterization. non_fatal_censors_fatal Logical. Controls semi-competing risks behavior multiple TTE endpoints present. TRUE, censoring non-fatal endpoint can censor subsequent TTE endpoints according package's semi-competing-risk rules. FALSE, non-fatal censoring censor TTE endpoints. target_correlation Logical scalar. TRUE correlation_matrix NULL, numerical calibration step used search latent Gaussian correlation matrix whose transformed endpoints approximately match requested observed Pearson correlation matrix. FALSE, supplied correlation_matrix used directly latent Gaussian correlation matrix. value generally set TRUE, unless compelling reason otherwise. arm_mode Character string controlling number treatment arms determined. Must one : auto Infer number arms endpoint specifications. treatment-specific quantities supplied, control-mode used. full Force full multi-arm interpretation, even endpoint specifications omit treatment-specific effects. control Force control-mode. case, treatment-specific arguments trt_effect, trt_prob, trt_count allowed. value generally set "auto". calibration_control Named list controls correlation calibration routine used target_correlation = TRUE. Common controls include Monte Carlo size, tolerance, iteration caps, constraints ensure valid correlation matrix. See calibration_control detail.

title: Solve exponential rate parameters from target observed event probabilities — rate_from_prob
code: rate_from_prob(   target_prob,   mode = c("simple", "admin", "semi-competing"),   event_rate = NULL,   admin_time = NULL,   fatal_event_rate = NULL,   fatal_censor_rate = NULL,   nonfatal_event_rate = NULL )

title: Solve exponential rate parameters from target observed event probabilities — rate_from_prob
text: target_prob numeric scalar (0, 1) giving target observed event probability. meaning depends mode: "simple", desired probability event observed independent random censoring; "admin", desired probability event occurs administrative follow-limit; "semi-competing", desired approximate probability secondary non-fatal event observed first. mode Character string indicating probability--rate relationship use. Must one : simple One time--event endpoint independent exponential censoring. admin One time--event endpoint administrative censoring . semi-competing Approximate semi-competing risks calculation secondary non-fatal endpoint. event_rate Numeric scalar giving exponential event rate \(\lambda_e\). Used mode = "simple". admin_time Numeric scalar giving administrative censoring time horizon \(\). Used mode = "admin". fatal_event_rate Numeric scalar giving event rate primary fatal event, denoted \(\lambda_{e1}\). Used mode = "semi-competing". fatal_censor_rate Numeric scalar giving independent censoring rate fatal endpoint, denoted \(\lambda_{c1}\). Used mode = "semi-competing". nonfatal_event_rate Numeric scalar giving event rate secondary non-fatal endpoint, denoted \(\lambda_{e2}\). Used mode = "semi-competing".

title: Solve exponential rate parameters from target observed event probabilities — rate_from_prob
text: numeric scalar giving solved rate parameter. Depending mode, : censoring rate ("simple"), event rate ("admin"), approximate non-fatal censoring rate ("semi-competing").

title: Solve exponential rate parameters from target observed event probabilities — rate_from_prob
text: Depending mode, function solves : independent exponential censoring rate ("simple"), exponential event rate pure administrative censoring ("admin"), approximate censoring rate secondary non-fatal endpoint semi-competing risks setting ("semi-competing"). intended design-stage helper choosing rate parameters calling makeData. cases covered. complicated settings, iteration may necessary.

title: Solve exponential rate parameters from target observed event probabilities — rate_from_prob
text: Assume single event time \(T \sim \mathrm{Exp}(\lambda_e)\) independent censoring time \(C \sim \mathrm{Exp}(\lambda_c)\). probability observing event $$ P(T < C) = \frac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_e + \lambda_c}. $$ Given \(\lambda_e\) target probability \(p\), function solves censoring rate \(\lambda_c\): $$ \lambda_c = \frac{\lambda_e}{p} - \lambda_e. $$ mode, returned value required independent censoring rate.

title: Solve exponential rate parameters from target observed event probabilities — rate_from_prob
text: Assume single event time \(T \sim \mathrm{Exp}(\lambda_e)\) fixed administrative censoring time \(> 0\). probability observing event time \(\) $$ P(T \le ) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_e }. $$ Given \(\) target probability \(p\), function solves event rate \(\lambda_e\): $$ \lambda_e = -\frac{\log(1 - p)}{}. $$ mode, returned value required event rate.

title: Solve exponential rate parameters from target observed event probabilities — rate_from_prob
text: mode uses low-correlation independent-clock approximation secondary non-fatal event semi-competing risks setting. Let: \(\lambda_{e1}\) fatal event rate, \(\lambda_{c1}\) censoring rate fatal endpoint, \(\lambda_{e2}\) non-fatal event rate, \(\lambda_{c2}\) censoring rate non-fatal endpoint. approximation used package vignette, probability secondary non-fatal event observed first $$ p \approx \frac{\lambda_{e2}} {\lambda_{e1} + \lambda_{c1} + \lambda_{e2} + \lambda_{c2}}. $$ Given \(p\), \(\lambda_{e1}\), \(\lambda_{c1}\), \(\lambda_{e2}\), function solves \(\lambda_{c2}\): $$ \lambda_{c2} = \frac{\lambda_{e2}}{p} - \left(\lambda_{e1} + \lambda_{c1} + \lambda_{e2}\right). $$ mode, returned value approximate non-fatal censoring rate. approximation, realized observed non-fatal event probability makeData may differ dependence, censoring, fatal-event logic fully applied.

title: Solve exponential rate parameters from target observed event probabilities — rate_from_prob

code: ## Continuous + binary example ep1 <- list(   endpoint_type = "continuous",   baseline_mean = 10,   sd            = 2,   trt_effect    = -1 )  ep2 <- list(   endpoint_type = "binary",   baseline_prob = 0.30,   trt_prob      = 0.45 )  R2 <- corr_make(   num_endpoints = 2,   values = rbind(c(1, 2, 0.2)) )  sim_obj <- makeData(   correlation_matrix    = R2,   sample_size_per_group = 500,   SEED                  = 1,   endpoint_details      = list(ep1, ep2) )  ss <- summary(sim_obj)  ##
